title: A Lark for the Ages—Or So I Thought
author: Shara (Sinor) Johnson
summary: Inspired by Narratively Classic “The Teenage Prank That’s Lasted 60 Years,” we asked you over at to send us mini flash essays about the best pranks you ever pulled. As always, you met the challenge with aplomb. We laughed our way through tales of a “random joy” takeover on a bus, a practical joke involving a pregnant woman and an elevator, another featuring a gorilla suit and a fateful camping trip. Ultimately, we went with this lovely little piece from Shara Sinor that made us smile, giggle and keep an eye on our cupboards all at once.
full_text:
Inspired by Narratively Classic “The Teenage Prank That’s Lasted 60 Years,” we asked you over at Narratively Academy to send us mini flash essays about the best pranks you ever pulled. As always, you met the challenge with aplomb. We laughed our way through tales of a “random joy” takeover on a bus, a practical joke involving a pregnant woman and an elevator, another featuring a gorilla suit and a fateful camping trip. Ultimately, we went with this lovely little piece from Shara Sinor that made us smile, giggle and keep an eye on our cupboards all at once. 

Illustration by David Huang/Narratively archive 

My favorite childhood holiday: April Fool’s Day. Some of my victims took clever revenge, but my mom only, “Oh, Shara!” or a shriek, then laughter.

She crafted home-cooked meals every day, put together from a well-stocked pantry packed with canned goods: soups, meats, vegetables, evaporated and condensed milk, sauces. One productive Fool’s Eve, I took the label off every single can. After enduring a morning spring-loaded with pranks, and choking on the brownies I’d baked with hickory-smoked salt, Mom announced she’d make casserole for lunch. I hid in the next room snickering, waiting for, “Shara!”

Silence.

Then the motor of the electric can opener. Spoons clanging inside pots. Oven door closing.

Finally the call for lunch — now she would let me have it.

Nothing.

Delicious casserole.

For two days, she swished around the kitchen in her apron while I writhed with anxiety: Was she plotting a cold dish of revenge? Panicked, I taped my redemption inside the cupboard door: I had penned a symbol on each can and created a corresponding key explaining what they all stood for! Mom did not remark on it. No “thank you,” no “whew.” 

After a couple days, I opened the cupboard to see if she’d been buying new cans with labels.

No new cans. No list on the door. My redemption was stolen!

“Mom! I made a key for you. Really!”

She smiled at me, paused (undoubtedly to savor my dismay). “I know,” she said. “I threw it away.”

“What?!”

“I’ve made a game of it. I hold and shake each can to guess what’s inside. I’m pretty good at it.”

It was a resounding flop as a “prank” — my victim was delighted! Instead it was the best mirror: It wasn’t my grin reflected, but my inherited spirit in the form of Mom shaking cans like maracas.

Shara Sinor writes creative and narrative nonfiction from her home in the Rocky Mountains of Colorado. She is also a photographer and maintains a travel blog of her adventures abroad and throughout Colorado.
